mnemonics.title: AVL Rotation Cases
mnemonics.content:
L-L: Left-heavy with Left child → Right rotate
R-R: Right-heavy with Right child → Left rotate
L-R: Left-heavy with Right child → Left then Right
R-L: Right-heavy with Left child → Right then Left

mnemonics.title: Red-Black Properties
mnemonics.content:
ROOT is always BLACK
Every node is RED or BLACK
Leaves (NIL) are always BLACK
RED nodes have BLACK children
All paths have equal BLACK height

mnemonics.title: B-Tree Properties
mnemonics.content:
All leaves at SAME level
Keys SORTED within node
Node has between t-1 and 2t-1 keys
Root has at least 1 key (if not leaf)
Children act as separators between keys

acronyms.usage: Named after inventors of the first self-balancing BST
Multi-way search tree optimized for disk access
Virtual black leaf nodes in Red-Black trees for boundary conditions

visualTips.visualization: Draw tree with BF written inside each node. + means left taller, - means right taller, 0 means balanced. Check after each insertion.
Think of rotation as pulling the root down in the direction of the lighter subtree. LL: pull root down-right. RR: pull root down-left.
Root always black. Red nodes are like warnings - they cannot have red children. Black nodes provide the height guarantee.
Horizontal tree with multiple keys per node. Keys act like fence posts dividing children. All leaves at bottom level.

analogies.analogy: Like a perfectly balanced scale where weights can differ by at most one unit on either side.
Like a city with some traffic rules (no red-red) but more flexible than strict height limits.
Like a bookshelf with books sorted - each shelf (node) holds multiple books (keys), minimizing trips to storage (disk).
Like rebalancing a mobile sculpture - moving weights around while maintaining the visual order.
